perturbation.original_text: 15. Compliance With The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act And Export Control And Antiboycott Laws Neither GINARES or HPIL ET or any representative of GINARES or HPIL ET in its capacity as such has violated the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act or the anticorruption laws of any jurisdiction where GINARES or HPIL ET does business. Each of GINARES and HPIL ET has at all times complied with all legal requirements relating to export control and trade sanctions or embargoes. Either GINARES or HPIL ET have violated the antiboycott prohibitions contained in 50 U.S.C. Sections 2401 et seq. or taken any action that can be penalized under Section 999 of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. Compliance With The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act And Export Control And Antiboycott Laws Neither GINARES or HPIL ET or any representative of GINARES or HPIL ET in its capacity as such has violated the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act or the anticorruption laws of any jurisdiction where GINARES or HPIL ET does business. Each of GINARES and HPIL ET has at all times complied with all legal requirements relating to export control and trade sanctions or embargoes.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the clause 'Either GINARES or HPIL ET have violated the antiboycott prohibitions contained in 50 U.S.C. Sections 2401 et seq. or taken any action that can be penalized under Section 999 of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended' weakens the clause, decreasing compliance with US anti-boycott laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 50 U.S. Code § 4607 - Criminal penalties for violations
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the explicit statement regarding compliance with antiboycott prohibitions under 50 U.S.C. Sections 2401 et seq. and Section 999 of the Internal Revenue Code, the modified text creates ambiguity regarding adherence to these laws. 50 U.S.C. § 4607 outlines penalties for violating regulations issued under the Export Control Reform Act of 2018, which includes antiboycott regulations. The omission implies a lack of commitment to these laws, potentially contradicting the legal requirement to comply and disclose violations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly stated compliance with antiboycott prohibitions, while the changed text omits this statement. The Law Explanation suggests this omission creates ambiguity regarding adherence to antiboycott laws. Given that the original text included an explicit commitment to avoid violating antiboycott laws, and the modified text removes that commitment, this is a significant weakening of compliance. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not Found' error and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None,' the perturbation and law explanations clarify that omitting the antiboycott clause weakens the agreement and introduces a legal loophole because the original text contained a statement regarding compliance. This is an omission that weakens legal protections, therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Arbitration Any and all disputes or controversies between the Parties arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled by arbitration in accordance with the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce; provided, a Party may seek a temporary restraining order, preliminary injunction, or other provisional judicial relief if in its judgment such action is necessary to avoid irreparable damage or to preserve the status quo. Despite any such action for provisional relief, the Parties will continue to participate in good faith in the procedures specified in this Section 16. Each Party shall appoint one arbitrator who shall mutually appoint a third arbitrator who shall be the sole arbitrator for the proceeding. The arbitration shall be held, and any award shall be rendered, in Paris (France), in the English language. The award may include reimbursement of the costs of the arbitration (including, without limitation, reasonable attorney fees) to the prevailing Party or a portion of such costs as determined by the arbitrator. An award of the arbitrator shall be final and binding on the Parties and judgment upon the award rendered by the arbitrator may be entered by any court having jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Arbitration Any and all disputes or controversies between the Parties arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled by arbitration in accordance with the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce; provided, a Party may seek a temporary restraining order, preliminary injunction, or other provisional judicial relief if in its judgment such action is necessary to avoid irreparable damage or to preserve the status quo. Each Party shall appoint one arbitrator who shall mutually appoint a third arbitrator who shall be the sole arbitrator for the proceeding. The arbitration shall be held, and any award shall be rendered, in Paris (France), in the English language. The award may include reimbursement of the costs of the arbitration (including, without limitation, reasonable attorney fees) to the prevailing Party or a portion of such costs as determined by the arbitrator. An award of the arbitrator shall be final and binding on the Parties and judgment upon the award rendered by the arbitrator may be entered by any court having jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of "Despite any such action for provisional relief, the Parties will continue to participate in good faith in the procedures specified in this Section 16" may allow a party to halt the arbitration by seeking provisional relief. This can contradict general good faith standards required in contractual relationships.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) 104.1304 Obligation of good faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law (NRS 104.1304) implies a general obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. By removing the clause that requires continued participation in arbitration even when provisional judicial relief is sought, the edited agreement allows a party to potentially stall or derail the arbitration process without explicitly violating the agreement, which then goes against the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the phrase "Despite any such action for provisional relief, the Parties will continue to participate in good faith in the procedures specified in this Section 16" from the arbitration clause creates a contradiction under Nevada law, specifically NRS 104.1304, which implies an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. By removing the requirement to continue arbitration even when seeking provisional relief, the modified text enables a party to potentially stall or derail the arbitration process. This undermines the good faith obligation as it provides an opportunity for strategic maneuvering to avoid or delay arbitration, contradicting the intent of NRS 104.1304.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Notices All notices and other communications required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given (a) when delivered in person, (b) five (5) business days after being sent by registered or certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, (c) when dispatched by electronic facsimile transmission (with confirmation of successful transmission), or (d) one (1) business day after having been dispatched by an internationally recognized overnight courier service, in each case to the appropriate Party at the address or facsimile number specified below: If to HPIL ET: HPIL ENERGYTECH Inc. Attn.: Louis Bertoli, President and CEO 7075 Gratiot Road, Suite One Saginaw, Michigan 48609 United States of America Facsimile No.: 001(248)750-1016 with a copy (which shall not constitute notice) to the following e-mail addresses: info@hpilenergytech.com If to the GINARES: GINARES GROUP AG Attn.: Peter Zu Sayn-Wittgenstein, President and CEO Churerstrasse 47 Pfaeffikon 8808 Switzerland Facsimile No.: +41(55)511-0810 with a copy (which shall not constitute notice) to the following e-mail address: info@ginares.com Any Party hereto may change its address or facsimile number for the purposes of this Section 13 by giving notice as provided herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Notices All notices and other communications required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given (a) when delivered in person or (b) when dispatched by electronic mail, in each case to the appropriate Party at the address or e-mail specified below: If to HPIL ET: HPIL ENERGYTECH Inc. Attn.: Louis Bertoli, President and CEO 7075 Gratiot Road, Suite One Saginaw, Michigan 48609 United States of America E-mail: info@hpilenergytech.com If to the GINARES: GINARES GROUP AG Attn.: Peter Zu Sayn-Wittgenstein, President and CEO Churerstrasse 47 Pfaeffikon 8808 Switzerland E-mail: info@ginares.com Any Party hereto may change its address or facsimile number for the purposes of this Section 13 by giving notice as provided herein.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting the methods of providing notice and removing acknowledgment conditions such as return receipt, it weakens the clause. This can be problematic under Nevada law, particularly concerning breach of contract remedies where proof of notice is crucial.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 104.1202 defines 'notice' and outlines when an entity has 'knowledge' of a fact. The altered notice clause removes the certainty provided by methods like registered mail with return receipt, making it harder to prove effective notice under Nevada law. This directly impacts the enforceability of the contract if one party claims they did not receive required notices.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies multiple methods for delivering notices, including registered or certified mail with return receipt requested, providing proof of delivery. The changed text removes these methods, leaving only in-person delivery or electronic mail. This omission weakens the ability to prove that notice was duly given, which can impact the enforceability of the contract under Nevada law. NRS 104.1202, as referenced by the Law Explanation, defines 'notice' and outlines when an entity has 'knowledge' of a fact. By removing methods with confirmation of receipt, the changed text potentially contradicts the legal requirement of ensuring effective notice, making it more difficult to establish knowledge as defined by Nevada law, particularly concerning breach of contract remedies.
